fundamental.thesis: AU Small Finance Bank is a high-growth small finance bank transitioning to a universal bank, with a strong retail-secured and commercial banking franchise that grew its gross loan portfolio 21% YoY to ₹1,40,327 cr in FY26, significantly outpacing estimated private sector credit growth of ~13%. Revenue grew 14.7% YoY to ₹12,091 cr in FY26 while PAT grew 25% YoY to ₹2,641 cr (margin ~21.9%), with Q4FY26 PAT surging 65% YoY to ₹832 cr — though this reflects seasonal strength and some credit-cost tailwind (60bps in Q4 vs guided 90bps for FY27), so the trajectory is accelerating at the headline level but with a cautionary note that full-year ROA of 1.6% lagged Q4's 1.8%. Trailing P/E of 27.8× sits 42% above the sector median of 19.6× and P/B of 3.6× is at the 75th percentile among peers — for a bank with ROE implicitly ~14-15% (PAT ₹2,641 cr on ~₹18,000 cr book), this P/B premium is partly justified by growth but leaves the stock fairly valued to mildly expensive relative to peers, especially since forward P/E of 16× implies significant earnings growth that hinges on credit cost discipline. The key risk is that credit costs for FY27 are guided higher at ~90bps versus Q4FY26's 60bps, and the digital unsecured / credit card book (down 10% YoY) has been a multi-quarter drag — any re-acceleration of stress in the unsecured segment or NIM compression from higher funding costs could pressure the 1.8% ROA target management is banking on.

fundamental.thesis: Yes Bank is a mid-sized private sector bank in India, now stabilised post-reconstruction with SMBC (Sumitomo Mitsui Banking Corporation) as its largest shareholder at 24.9%, providing a credible institutional anchor and governance upgrade. The bank delivered FY26 PAT of ₹3,476 cr, up 44.5% YoY — a strong absolute print — but the trajectory is partially supported by one-off Security Receipt gains (₹1,559 cr in FY26) and RIDF rundown tailwinds rather than purely organic NII expansion; NIM improved only 20 bps YoY to 2.6%, which remains well below the 3.25–3.5% medium-term target, and retail advances growth was a muted 4.7% YoY despite disbursement acceleration. Trailing P/E of 20.6× sits above the 12–18× bank median, making it look optically expensive for a bank with ROE of only 7.1% and P/B of 1.47×; applying the P/B + ROE rule (P/B < 1.5 with ROE > 12% = cheap), YES Bank fails the ROE test decisively — ROE at 7.1% is well below the 12% threshold, so P/B of 1.47× is not cheap and the stock is fairly to moderately expensive on a quality-adjusted basis. The biggest risk is that the PAT growth narrative is substantially backstopped by non-recurring Security Receipt recoveries and RIDF rundown rather than sustainable NIM and fee-income expansion, meaning FY27 consensus earnings growth could disappoint if slippage ratios in retail remain elevated (2.93%) and NIM expansion stalls.

fundamental.thesis: LIC Housing Finance is India's second-largest housing finance company (HFC), backed by LIC of India's franchise, focused on individual home loans (~80% of book) with a growing non-housing segment; its Rs. 3.2 lakh cr loan portfolio and 303 marketing offices give it a wide retail distribution moat. FY26 full-year revenue grew a modest +2.6% YoY to Rs. 28,765 cr and PAT grew +3.1% YoY to Rs. 5,595 cr — growth has been slow and stable rather than accelerating, with Q4FY26 showing slightly better momentum (PAT +9% YoY) though this follows a weak base; however, NIM compressed to 2.68% in FY26 from 2.73% in FY25, and impairment on financial instruments surged 112% to Rs. 554 cr, both meaningful deterioration signals. At a trailing P/E of 5.4x (vs Financials sector median ~17x) and P/B of 0.73x against ROE of 14.4%, LICHSGFIN screens as deeply cheap in absolute and peer-relative terms — ranked P0 cheapest in its 18-stock sector peer group — but the discount is partially warranted given NIM compression, rising credit costs, and high financial leverage (D/E ~643x). The key risk is further NIM erosion combined with a continued spike in Stage 2/3 assets as the high-leverage balance sheet leaves little cushion for credit-cost surprises in a rising-crude, rate-sensitive macro environment.

fundamental.thesis: M&M Financial Services is a leading rural/semi-urban NBFC with a dominant franchise in vehicle financing (wheels segment) and a growing presence in mortgages and SME lending, operating in a structural financialization theme. FY26 full-year PAT grew 19% YoY to ₹2,782 cr (or ~30% adjusted for management overlays), with Q4FY26 PAT surging 55% YoY to ₹873 cr — a clear acceleration from earlier quarters (Q4FY25 PAT was ₹457 cr); NIM expanded 101 bps YoY to 7.5% in Q4FY26, and asset quality improved meaningfully with GS2+GS3 at 8.2% (8-year low) and GS3 declining to 3.4% — all key NBFC KPIs trending in the right direction. At a trailing P/E of 14.0×, the stock trades at a 16.6% discount to its Financials sector median (16.8×) and sits at P33 among 19 sector peers; P/B of 1.52× with ROE of 11.9% is borderline on the cheap-fair boundary per the bank/NBFC framework (P/B < 1.5 with ROE > 12% = cheap), placing it at fair-to-slightly-cheap, and forward P/E of 10.45× well below trailing implies the market is pricing in continued earnings growth. The biggest risk is the ₹482 cr management overlay created for FY26 (plus an additional ₹217 cr in Q4FY26 for geopolitical and adverse monsoon headwinds in FY27) signalling management's own caution about near-term asset quality deterioration in the rural economy — if monsoon disappoints or rural stress deepens, credit costs could breach the guided 1.3–1.7% band and compress FY27 earnings materially.

fundamental.thesis: SBI Cards & Payment Services is India's second-largest credit card issuer with ~18.8% cards-in-force market share, operating as an NBFC-UL under RBI oversight with SBI's 68.9% promoter backing providing a strong liability franchise. Q4FY26 revenue grew 7% YoY to ₹5,187 cr and full-year FY26 revenue rose 10% YoY to ₹19,900 cr, with PAT growing 13–14% YoY aided by improving credit quality (GNPA down 67 bps YoY to 2.41%), though receivables growth has decelerated sharply to just 2% YoY — a key leading indicator of future revenue stress — and new account additions declined 12% YoY in FY26, signalling slowing acquisition momentum. At trailing P/E of 26×, SBICARD trades at a 53% premium to the Financials sector median of 17×, ranking P68 among peers; however, P/B of 3.6× against ROE of 14.7% is elevated (the P/B-ROE ratio does not justify a 'cheap' tag — ROE would need to be 20%+ to support this P/B), making the stock fairly valued at best, and the forward P/E of 16.3× suggests the market is pricing in earnings recovery but still at a premium to sector peers. The single biggest risk is that revolver balances — the highest-yielding receivables — are on a structural downward trend, which, combined with yield compression (NIM at 11%, yield at 16.3% and falling) and elevated credit costs (8.6% gross write-off rate), could compress NIM further and stall PAT growth if the EMI book does not compensate adequately.

fundamental.thesis: HDFC Asset Management is India's second-largest mutual fund manager by AUM (₹9.3 trillion QAAUM, ~13% market share), benefiting directly from the structural financialization of household savings via SIP inflows that hit an all-time high of ₹321 bn/month in March 2026. Full-year FY26 revenue grew 18% YoY to ₹412 cr with PAT up 16% to ₹286 cr at a healthy 61.9% PAT margin; however Q4 FY26 standalone PAT dipped 2% YoY to ₹62.3 cr, and the trajectory shows mild deceleration from 9M FY26 PAT growth of 23% YoY to a full-year 16%, suggesting margin and earnings momentum is softening at the margin. At a trailing P/E of 37.5× (P90 within Financials sector peers, +119% premium to sector median of 17.1×) and P/B of 11.6× (P100 in sector), the stock is expensive vs. its Financials peer group — though the premium is partially justified by an exceptional ROE of 32.9% and asset-light business model; the more relevant AMC/wealth-management peer set would warrant a higher multiple, but no direct listed AMC peer data is available here. The single biggest near-term risk is the new Base Expense Ratio (BER) regulation effective April 1, 2026, which management guided will compress operating margins by 3–5 bps on existing flows and remove exit-load distribution income, potentially capping earnings growth below the 15–20% trajectory priced into the current forward P/E of 28.5×.

fundamental.thesis: Poonawalla Fincorp is a fast-growing NBFC backed by the Poonawalla Group, pivoting from a clean personal-loan franchise toward a diversified retail lending platform spanning gold loans, commercial vehicles, education, and shopkeeper loans. The latest quarter (Q4FY26) shows NII at ₹1,276 cr (+78.5% YoY) and PAT at ₹255 cr (+310% YoY from a very low base of ₹62 cr in Q4FY25), with FY26 full-year PAT of ₹542 cr reversing a ₹98 cr loss in FY25 — trajectory is strongly accelerating, though the base effect is exceptional and the multi-quarter PAT path (₹18.7 cr → ₹62.3 cr → ₹150 cr → ₹255 cr) shows genuine sequential improvement, not a one-off. Trailing P/E of 57.7× is at the P95 percentile among 22 sector peers and sits 214% above the sector median of 18.4×; the forward P/E of 16.5× signals market consensus of steep earnings normalisation ahead, but ROE of only 5.9% and P/B of 3.1× together flag that the stock is expensive in absolute terms — P/B > 3× with ROE well below 12% is a textbook expensive signal for an NBFC. The biggest risk is that rapid AUM growth (69.4% YoY to ₹60,348 cr) has been funded by sharply higher leverage (debt/equity of 470×; leverage ratio 4.67×), and with 50% of the portfolio unsecured and credit costs still at 2.51%, any macro or credit-cycle stress could compress ROA/ROE significantly and test the expensive valuation.

fundamental.thesis: Sundaram Finance is a well-regarded South India-based NBFC with a dominant franchise in commercial vehicle (CV), tractor, and construction equipment financing, with FY26 consolidated AUM reaching ₹59,908 cr (+16.4% YoY) and standalone PAT of ₹1,834 cr (+19% YoY) — growth has been steady and broad-based, with tractors (+29%), MHCV (+14%), and cars (+11%) offsetting a weak construction equipment segment (-7% YoY). The quarterly trajectory from official filings shows accelerating momentum: Q3FY25 revenue of ₹1,086 cr grew to ₹1,274 cr in Q3FY26 (~17% YoY) and ₹1,342 cr in Q4FY26, while PAT margin has expanded from roughly 41-42% of revenue (standalone quarterly) toward the FY26 full-year standalone PAT margin of 25.3% (investor presentation basis); critically, asset quality improved in H2FY26 with net Stage 3 declining to 0.69% from 0.75% YoY, though gross Stage 3 had risen through 9MFY26 (1.91% at Dec-25 vs 1.70% Dec-24), suggesting H1 stress is now stabilising. At a trailing P/E of 22.1× (P59 in Financials sector, +13% above median 19.6×) and forward P/E of 18.7× (P82, +42% above median 13.1×), valuation is fair-to-expensive for a NBFC — P/B of 3.0× against ROE of 14.7% sits in expensive territory by the P/B+ROE framework (P/B > 3× with ROE below 15% is a stretched signal), and at only 2% of its 52-week range the stock is near a 52-week low, which may reflect the market already pricing in this valuation concern. The single biggest risk is that gross NPA momentum resumes if rural/CV credit stress returns or if state government payment delays to contractors persist, which could compress NIM and force higher provisioning just as the exceptional Labour Code charge (₹75 cr) has already dented reported earnings quality.

fundamental.thesis: Max Financial Services (MFSL) is a listed holding company whose primary asset is Axis Max Life Insurance (AMLI), one of India's top private life insurers with ~10.4% private market share and strong new business momentum — Individual Adjusted FYP grew 19% YoY to ₹9,885 cr in FY26, ahead of the private industry's 12%, and Value of New Business (VNB) grew 26% YoY to ₹2,647 cr with New Business Margin expanding to 25.2% from 24.0%; however, consolidated PAT collapsed 74% YoY to ₹84 cr in FY26 (from ₹327 cr) driven by fair-value/mark-to-market losses on investments, and the quarterly PAT trajectory is deeply erratic (Q1FY26: ₹70 cr, Q2FY26: ₹4 cr, Q3FY26: ₹37 cr, Q4FY26: -₹28 cr), reflecting the volatility of insurance accounting rather than a stable earnings stream. Valuation is unambiguously expensive: trailing P/E of 682.7× versus the Financials sector median of 12–18×, forward P/E of 102.3× still at P100 (most expensive) among 24 sector peers, P/B of 20.3× against ROE of only 1.6% — a textbook value-trap signal — and EV/EBITDA of 69.1× at +161% premium to sector median; the market is pricing in a sharp multi-year earnings recovery from the insurance franchise's embedded value, but this is entirely contingent on equity market stability and operating leverage materialising. The single biggest risk is that solvency at AMLI has declined to 194% (approaching regulatory comfort levels), combined with a SEBI show-cause notice pending resolution, the cancelled Q4FY26 earnings call (a governance red flag for a midcap), high policyholder opex ratios, and the proposed merger of MFSL into AMLI pending regulatory approval — structural uncertainty that makes near-term earnings forecasting unreliable.

fundamental.thesis: Persistent Systems is a mid-cap IT services company with a strong focus on software product engineering, digital transformation, and AI-led services, occupying a niche position between large Indian IT primes and boutique consultancies. Revenue has grown consistently over the available quarters — Q4FY26 revenue of ₹2,758 cr represents approximately +21% YoY growth vs Q4FY25 ₹2,280 cr, with PAT growing from ₹396 cr to ₹529 cr (+34% YoY), and PAT margin expanding from ~17.4% to ~19.2%, suggesting healthy operating leverage and a positive trajectory rather than deceleration. However, at a trailing P/E of 42.5× — a staggering +160% premium to the sector peer median of 16.3× and ranked P100 (most expensive) among NIFTY 100 IT peers — the stock is unambiguously expensive relative to peers, with the forward P/E of 28.9× implying the market is already pricing in substantial earnings acceleration that must be delivered; the EV/EBITDA at 28× is at sector median (P50), which is the one valuation metric that does not scream expensive. The biggest risk is that the AI disruption structural headwind, combined with a richly priced growth multiple and the high reported Debt/Equity of 6.09×, leaves minimal margin of safety if quarterly growth momentum slows even modestly — and the lack of actual earnings call/presentation content in the filings prevents verification of deal TCV, guidance tone, or management confidence level.

fundamental.thesis: Mphasis is a mid-tier Indian IT services company with a strong focus on Banking & Financial Services (BFS) and direct business, competing against peers like Coforge and Persistent in niche digital transformation. FY26 full-year revenue grew 6.7% YoY in constant currency, with Q4FY26 accelerating to +7.1% YoY (₹3,107 cr) and EPS rising to ₹26.70 vs ₹23.13 in Q3FY25 — a modestly positive trajectory, though the ITO service line contracted 21.6% YoY due to deliberate ATM business scale-down, and the Logistics & Transportation vertical collapsed 54.3% YoY, partially masking the underlying BFS/Insurance strength (+17% and +46% YoY respectively). At a trailing P/E of 24.7× against the sector median of 22–30×, Mphasis sits at P67 among IT peers — a mild premium to median (19×) but well below high-growth peers Coforge and Persistent; forward P/E of 18.3× implies earnings growth is expected, and EV/EBITDA at 14.7× is at the sector peer median, making the stock broadly fairly valued. The biggest risk is that record TCV of $2.1Bn (+68% YoY) and a 69% AI-led pipeline fail to convert to revenue at the pace implied by FY27 guidance of high single-digit to low double-digit growth, especially if macro uncertainty delays BFS client decision cycles or the structural AI disruption headwind compresses deal pricing faster than management currently anticipates.

fundamental.thesis: Oracle Financial Services Software (OFSS) is a niche banking-technology product company — majority-owned by Oracle Corp — that licenses its FLEXCUBE core-banking platform and related fintech products to banks globally, giving it a software-economics moat with ~34% PAT margins and near-100% FCF conversion. FY26 full-year consolidated revenue grew +12% YoY to ₹7,672 cr and PAT grew +11% YoY to ₹2,639 cr, with Q4FY26 particularly strong at +20% revenue and +31% net income YoY; the multi-quarter trajectory is stable-to-accelerating at the revenue line, though a one-off ₹766m Labour Code provision in FY26 modestly dented margins — underlying margin quality remains high at ~34% PAT and ~41% Q4 net margin, and RPO (Remaining Performance Obligations) of ₹7,761 cr at Mar-26 is up 9.2% QoQ, pointing to near-term revenue visibility. At a trailing P/E of 33.4× vs the IT sector median of ~24.7×, OFSS trades at a +35% premium to peers and sits at P88 in its sector cohort, with P/B at 11.2× (P100 in sector) — the premium is partially justified by its 32.6% ROE, near-zero debt, and product-software margins that are structurally superior to services peers, but the stock at 88% of its 52-week range leaves very limited margin of safety for any guidance miss. The principal risk is AI-driven disruption of the banking-technology stack, where cloud-native challengers and GenAI-based core-banking alternatives could commoditise FLEXCUBE licensing over a 3–5 year horizon, while near-term the lack of explicit management guidance and a high absolute valuation make the stock vulnerable to any macro-driven de-rating of Indian IT multiples.

fundamental.thesis: Alkem Laboratories is India's 5th-largest pharma company by market share, operating a domestic branded-generics franchise (₹9,851 cr, +10% YoY) alongside a fast-growing international business (US: ₹2,984 cr, +20% YoY; Non-US: ₹1,696 cr, +27% YoY), with FY26 consolidated revenue of ₹14,712 cr (+13.5% YoY) and EBITDA margin expanding 100 bps to 20.4% — a genuinely positive trajectory, though FY26 PAT growth was muted at +6.3% YoY (₹2,302 cr, 15.6% margin) due to ₹1,748 cr net exceptional charges (gratuity liability + real estate impairment) that depress the reported number and should be stripped for clean assessment. At a trailing P/E of 27.4× against the sector median of 33.2× (P20 rank among peers) and EV/EBITDA of 20.2× vs peer median 22.2×, Alkem screens as cheap relative to its Healthcare peer set, with forward P/E of 23.1× confirming earnings growth is priced in and providing a reasonable entry case at mid-range of its 52-week band. The biggest risk to the thesis is the Daman formulations facility Form 483 (7 observations, April 2026) which — if it escalates to a Warning Letter — could disrupt US revenue, the highest-growth segment, combined with CEO transition uncertainty and the €180.7 mn Occlutech MedTech acquisition adding execution and leverage risk to what has been a clean pharma balance sheet.

fundamental.thesis: Glenmark Pharmaceuticals is a mid-cap Indian pharma company with diversified revenue across India, North America, Europe, and Emerging Markets, and a growing specialty pipeline anchored by RYALTRIS (now in 55 markets) and the landmark AbbVie/ISB 2001 deal worth up to USD 1.925B. Q4FY26 consolidated revenue grew 15.8% YoY to ₹3,771 cr with EBITDA margin of 20.2%, but PAT margin compressed to 7.6% — a deceleration from Q3FY26's 10.3% PAT margin — weighed down by antitrust settlements, exceptional items (₹180 cr standalone), and a standalone PAT turning to a loss of ₹20 cr; the FY26 full-year consolidated PAT grew ~30% YoY but is heavily distorted by one-offs, and the yfinance quarterly PAT figures show extreme volatility (₹610 cr in Q2FY26 down to ₹301 cr in Q4FY26) that reflects deferred licensing income recognition rather than clean operating earnings. Trailing P/E of 45.1× sits at P67 among sector peers and is above the sector median of 37.4×, making it modestly expensive on trailing earnings; however, EV/EBITDA of 15.6× is the cheapest in the sector (P0) at a 23% discount to the peer median of 20.2×, and the forward P/E of 22.3× — a steep discount to trailing — signals the market is pricing in a significant earnings normalisation once one-offs roll off, which tilts the overall valuation to fair. The single biggest risk is the high Debt/Equity of 12.78× combined with net debt of ~₹4,686 cr (per the financial results filing), which creates meaningful interest coverage pressure if North America revenue — which was down 23.7% YoY in 9MFY26 before recovering in Q4 — does not sustain its recovery trajectory.

fundamental.thesis: Laurus Labs is a vertically integrated Indian pharma company with a strong API/CDMO franchise and an ARV-heavy generics business, increasingly pivoting toward CDMO (now >30% of revenue vs 13% six years ago) and biologics as its next growth vectors. FY26 full-year revenue grew 23% YoY to ₹6,813 cr with PAT surging 148% YoY to ₹889 cr (PAT margin expanding 660 bps to 13.0%), driven by CDMO growing 36% and generics 18% — the trajectory is strongly accelerating off a low base, with EBITDA margins at a record 26.8%; however Q4 FY26 showed sequential softness in FDF (-26% QoQ) and CDMO small molecules were lumpy quarter-to-quarter, flagging execution timing risk. At a trailing P/E of 92x (vs pharma sector median of 28–40x) and EV/EBITDA of 44.8x (vs peer median 22.2x), the stock sits at P100 among its Healthcare sector peers on both P/E and forward P/E metrics and is trading at 98.7% of its 52-week range — the valuation is unambiguously expensive, pricing in near-flawless CDMO execution for multiple years ahead. The single biggest risk is that CDMO revenue is inherently lumpy and campaign-timing-dependent, and any slip in the late-stage pipeline conversion or the ₹3,000 cr capex ramp (Unit 7 greenfield, fermentation, peptide) could trigger a sharp de-rating from these stretched multiples.

fundamental.thesis: Biocon is a vertically integrated biopharma with a leading global biosimilars franchise (top-5 globally, 30+ products, $200B+ TAM), a generics business, and a CRDMO arm (Syngene), having recently completed the transformative acquisition and integration of Viatris' biosimilars business. FY26 revenue grew +11–14% YoY to ~₹17,270 cr (consolidated), driven by biosimilars (+16% YoY to ₹10,431 cr) and generics (+17% YoY), but PAT collapsed -55.6% YoY to ₹436 cr (before exceptionals), compressing PAT margin to just 2.2–2.5%, as generics EBITDA margins halved from 12% to 5% and CRDMO EBITDA declined 11% YoY — revenue is accelerating but earnings quality has severely deteriorated. At a trailing P/E of 147.6×, Biocon trades at a 227% premium to the Healthcare sector median of 45×, placing it at P100 (most expensive) among peers on trailing earnings, though the forward P/E of 35.5× (at P62) signals the market is pricing in a sharp earnings recovery — EV/EBITDA at 23.3× sits at the sector median (P50), making the enterprise valuation fair but the current earnings multiple unjustifiably expensive given a ROE of only 1.1%. The single biggest risk is that the margin recovery thesis fails to materialise in FY27 — generics utilisation improvement is uncertain, CRDMO faces client-specific headwinds, and with debt/equity at 42× and net debt of ~$1.1B, any further delay in earnings normalisation could make the elevated multiple untenable.

fundamental.thesis: Mankind Pharma is a leading domestic-focused Indian pharma company that acquired BSV Limited in October 2024, expanding into specialty/super-specialty segments including women's health, immunoglobulins, and complex biologics, while organically strengthening its chronic therapy franchise (cardiac +16.7% YoY, anti-diabetic +14.4% YoY, chronic share now ~40%). Full-year FY26 consolidated revenue grew 17% YoY to ₹14,278 cr, but PAT declined 3.4% YoY to ₹1,938 cr (margin compressed to 13.6%) due to higher depreciation and finance costs from the BSV acquisition and an expiring Sikkim tax exemption; Q4FY26 showed a strong recovery with PAT up 30.4% YoY and adjusted EBITDA margin of 27.1%, suggesting the BSV drag is stabilising. Trailing P/E of 50.5× sits at the top of the sector benchmark range (28–40× for pharma) and at P60 among peers on forward P/E, with EV/EBITDA of 28× at P70 among peers — premium pricing is partially justified by the raised FY27 guidance (double-digit top-line, 25.5–26.5% EBITDA margin) and a clean net cash position of ₹3,932 cr, but the elevated valuation leaves little room for error. The single biggest risk is the tax rate step-up from ~15–16% to 25–26% in FY27 as the Sikkim exemption ends, which will materially compress reported PAT and EPS even if operational performance meets guidance.

fundamental.thesis: Fortis Healthcare is one of India's top private hospital networks (hospital + diagnostics), with FY26 consolidated revenue of ₹9,128 cr (+17.3% YoY) and PAT of ₹1,064 cr (+31.5% YoY), reflecting strong operating leverage as EBITDA margins expanded 240 bps to 22.8% — a genuine multi-quarter improvement trend, not a one-quarter blip, with Q3FY26 EBITDA growing 34.8% YoY and Q4FY26 PAT up 44.2% YoY showing the trajectory is accelerating into year-end. Valuation is clearly expensive: trailing P/E of 69.3× sits 44.8% above the sector peer median of 47.8× (P82 in sector) and EV/EBITDA of 35.6× is 49.8% above the sector median of 23.8×; even on a forward P/E of 40.9× the stock prices in substantial earnings growth, leaving limited margin of safety — graded expensive. The key operational KPIs are solid (ARPOB +3.4% to ₹2.51 cr, occupied beds +15% YoY, FY27 guidance of 15%+ hospital revenue growth and 150 bps further margin expansion maintained) but FY27 guidance is 'maintained' not 'raised', occupancy actually dipped to 68% from 69% despite bed additions, international patient growth is slowing, and debt/equity has risen sharply to 34.15× with net debt up to ₹2,334 cr (net debt/EBITDA 1.09×) as inorganic acquisitions accelerate. The biggest risk is that an expensive multiple (P82 vs peers, EV/EBITDA 49% premium to median) gives no cushion if FY27 execution disappoints — any occupancy miss, acquisition integration drag, or CGHS/government pricing pressure in Punjab could trigger a sharp de-rating.

fundamental.thesis: Ajanta Pharma is a mid-cap specialty pharma company with a diversified business across Branded Generics (India, Africa, Asia) and US Generics, with strong market positions in chronic therapy segments and an increasingly meaningful US business. FY26 results (official filing, confidence 0.9) show revenue growing +17.3% YoY to ₹5,453 cr and PAT growing +14.8% YoY to ₹1,056 cr — trajectory is healthy and broadly stable across the year, with Q3FY26 even accelerating to +20% revenue YoY and PAT +18% YoY; the standout driver is US Generics at +49% YoY for full FY26, while Branded Generics India grew a solid +14% YoY, partially offset by Asia Branded Generics declining -1% YoY and a ₹103 cr mark-to-market forex loss weighing on reported EBITDA. At a trailing P/E of 35.4×, AJANTPHARM trades at a -21.5% discount to the Healthcare sector median of 45.1× and sits at the P25 percentile among 13 sector peers — comfortably within the pharma benchmark range of 28–40×, making it fair-to-cheap on earnings; forward P/E of 26.3× meaningfully below trailing signals the market is already pricing in earnings acceleration, and the 8.3× P/B is elevated but justified by a 25.4% ROE and 33% ROCE; however, the reported Debt/Equity of 5.75 is flagged as HIGH and warrants scrutiny (though the filing shows net cash of ₹1,641 cr, suggesting this D/E figure may include lease or other liabilities — nonetheless a watch item). The single biggest risk is the Income Tax search operations conducted at company premises in August 2025 (Section 158BC notice received February 2026 covering 2019–2025), whose financial impact is explicitly stated as not yet ascertainable — this is a meaningful governance and contingent-liability overhang for a mid-cap.

fundamental.thesis: Bharat Forge is India's largest forging company with a diversified portfolio spanning automotive (domestic CV, passenger car, exports), defence, aerospace, and industrial segments — the business is actively pivoting from pure-play auto forging toward high-value defence and aerospace contracts, which now represent a meaningful and growing share of revenues. On a consolidated basis, FY26 revenue grew 11% YoY to ₹16,812 cr, but the trajectory is uneven: standalone revenue actually declined 5.1% YoY to ₹8,396 cr due to severe North America CV market weakness (NA truck revenues down 51% YoY in Q3), and PAT has been materially impaired by ₹1,545 cr in exceptional items including an ₹450 cr KPTL e-mobility write-down; EBITDA margin is holding at ~17.4% on a consolidated basis but EU operations are at a skeletal 3.9% and US at 3.5%, indicating overseas subsidiaries are a drag. Valuation is unambiguously expensive: trailing P/E of 86× is more than double the Auto sector median of 18–28× and sits at P100 among peers, EV/EBITDA of 33.4× is 140% above the sector peer median of 13.9×, and P/B of 9.7× against an ROE of only 11.6% is difficult to justify — the stock is trading near its 52-week high at 88% of range, pricing in aggressive execution of the 25% India-ops growth target and the defence/aerospace ramp. The single biggest risk is non-delivery on the FY27 25% India growth guidance — any delay in ATAGS/CQB carbine execution, continued NA CV weakness, or further e-mobility impairments would trigger a sharp de-rating from these stretched multiples.

fundamental.thesis: MRF is India's largest tyre manufacturer by revenue and brand equity, operating as a near-monopoly in premium tyres with a dominant OEM and replacement market presence. FY26 full-year results (from official filings) show revenue of ₹31,149 cr (+10.5% YoY) and PAT of ₹2,426 cr (+29.5% YoY), with PAT growth meaningfully outpacing revenue growth — EBITDA margins expanded to ~15.9% from ~14.4% in FY25, indicating genuine operating leverage; at the quarterly level, Q3FY26 saw an exceptional PAT jump of +119% YoY partly due to a low base (Q3FY25 had a ₹77 cr one-off charge), so underlying normalised PAT growth is more moderate but still solidly positive. Trailing P/E of 21.6× sits comfortably below the Auto sector median of 30.5× (P33 in peer rank) and well within the 18–28× benchmark, EV/EBITDA of 10.4× is at a 20% discount to the sector median of 13.0×, and P/B of 2.5× is the cheapest in the peer set (P0); the stock is also near its 52-week low (4% of range), making the risk/reward entry more attractive — however the debt/equity of 15.29 is anomalously high and requires scrutiny (likely includes working capital facilities), and the forward P/E of 18.4× below trailing confirms the market expects continued earnings growth. The key risk is rising crude oil at $93/bbl — rubber and carbon black input costs are oil-linked, and sustained crude elevation could compress the margin gains of FY26, particularly given management provides no forward guidance to anchor expectations.

fundamental.thesis: Balkrishna Industries is a global niche leader in Off-Highway Tyres (OHT), with a presence in 160+ countries and a dominant position in agricultural and construction tyres; it is now extending into On-Highway (CV Radial, PCR) and Carbon Black segments targeting 2.2× revenue growth to ₹23,000 cr by FY30. FY26 (consolidated) delivered revenue growth of just +3.6% YoY to ₹10,823 cr while PAT declined sharply by -24.9% YoY to ₹1,243 cr — a multi-quarter trajectory of margin compression (EBITDA margin down ~252 bps to 22.4–22.7%, PAT margin down ~380 bps to 11.2–11.5%) driven by higher finance costs on a rising debt load (net debt ₹4,045 cr vs near net-cash in 9MFY25), exceptional items, and US tariff headwinds that cut Americas volumes ~30% YoY. At trailing P/E of 33.6× and EV/EBITDA of 19.5× the stock sits at the sector median on P/E but at P75 on EV/EBITDA (40% premium to sector median of 13.9×), placing it in 'fair-to-expensive' territory relative to Auto peers, though the forward P/E of 21.9× (significant discount to trailing) signals meaningful earnings recovery is already priced in by the market — the stock is near its 52-week low at 18% of range, reflecting current fundamental weakness rather than pure value. The single biggest risk is capex execution: ₹6,800 cr committed through FY29 has already flipped FCF negative (₹-754 cr FY26) and pushed net debt/EBITDA to 1.67×, and any demand shortfall — particularly in the US given ongoing tariff uncertainty — could further stress coverage ratios and delay the FY30 margin recovery thesis.

fundamental.thesis: Exide Industries is India's leading lead-acid battery manufacturer serving auto OEM, replacement, industrial (UPS, inverters), and solar segments, with a strategically important but capital-intensive lithium-ion cell manufacturing venture (EESL) in advanced commissioning. FY26 full-year revenue grew a modest +4.1% YoY to ₹17,269 cr with PAT up just +3.2% YoY to ₹1,111 cr (6.4% margin); however Q4 FY26 showed meaningful re-acceleration — standalone revenue +9.4% YoY and PAT +22.6% YoY to ₹312 cr — driven by Auto OEM (+25% YoY, second consecutive quarter) and domestic business +12.5% YoY, suggesting the trajectory is inflecting upward even as exports (~5% of revenue) remain a structural drag. Trading at a trailing P/E of 40.1× (P80 vs Auto sector peers, +9.9% premium to sector median of 36.5×) and EV/EBITDA of 18.6× (above sector median of 16.3×), the stock is expensive on earnings multiples — though the forward P/E of 23.9× implies the market is pricing in meaningful earnings recovery ahead, and P/B of 2.5× sits at the cheapest end of sector peers (P0), partially reflecting the low ROE of 6.2% which is depressed by the ₹4,802 cr EESL equity investment that is not yet generating returns. The single biggest risk is that the EESL gigafactory ramp-up is delayed or faces cost overruns — ₹1,400 cr more invested in FY27 — while commodities (sulfuric acid up 5× YoY, polypropylene tight) continue to compress core business margins, and the 40× trailing P/E leaves no room for execution disappointment.

fundamental.thesis: Tube Investments of India (TIINDIA) is a diversified industrial conglomerate spanning precision engineering tubes, metal formed products, EV mobility (TICMPL), and through its subsidiary CG Power, power systems and semiconductors — with a strong ROCE franchise of ~44% in its core engineering division. FY26 consolidated revenue grew 17.2% YoY to ₹22,847 cr (healthy top-line), but PAT grew only 6.1% YoY to ₹1,118 cr at a thin 4.9% margin, weighed down by an EV segment loss of ₹658 cr and a ₹137 cr fair-value hit on TICMPL CCPS — margins are compressing at the PAT level even as revenue accelerates, a deteriorating quality signal. Trailing P/E of 95× is dramatically above the Industrials sector median of 28–45×, and TIINDIA sits at P100 vs its auto/industrial peers on both trailing and forward P/E (+142% premium to median); EV/EBITDA of 27.4× is the more reliable metric for this capital-intensive business and is at P83 vs peers, still above the sector median — the stock is expensive even acknowledging forward P/E compression to 41× as earnings growth is priced in. The single biggest risk is the EV investment black hole: TICMPL has already consumed ~₹750 cr with another ₹500–750 cr planned, breakeven keeps being pushed out, and supply-chain disruptions cut Q4 three-wheeler production to 50% capacity — any further delay or write-down could materially impair consolidated PAT.

fundamental.thesis: Cummins India is a leading manufacturer of diesel and gas engines/generators with dominant positions in power generation, industrial, and distribution segments, benefiting from India's capex cycle and a fast-growing data centre market (30–35% of Power Gen domestic revenue in FY26). FY26 full-year revenue grew +18% YoY to ₹11,950 cr and PAT grew +22% YoY to ₹2,330 cr (19.5% margin), with PBT margin at 26%; however, Q3FY26 showed a -1% YoY revenue dip due to base-effect lumpiness in data centre execution and industrial segment weakness (-9% YoY), signalling that growth is uneven quarter-to-quarter rather than cleanly accelerating. At a trailing P/E of 67.7× versus the Industrials sector median of ~28–45×, and EV/EBITDA of 50.3× versus the peer median of 36.5×, the stock is unambiguously expensive — trading at P75 on P/E and P100 on P/B (20.2× vs peer median 5.0×) among sector peers, near its 52-week high (88.1% of range), pricing in a near-perfect execution of the data centre and domestic capex growth story. The biggest risk is a deceleration in data centre order flow or geopolitical disruption to exports, either of which would compress the premium multiple sharply given the stock's stretched valuation and moderating guidance tone for FY27.

fundamental.thesis: Supreme Industries is India's largest plastic piping and packaging products manufacturer, holding a dominant market share in PVC/CPVC piping systems with a debt-free balance sheet and a consistent 25%+ ROCE track record over 18 years. FY26 full-year revenue grew +7.4% YoY to ₹11,218 cr, but PAT growth was muted at just +1.6% YoY to ₹911 cr (8.1% margin), with PAT margins compressing from prior-year levels due to PVC price volatility (±32% swings within quarters), higher tax incidence, and a 144% surge in finance costs — the Q4FY26 quarter showed a strong sequential recovery with revenue up 16.5% YoY and EBITDA margin expanding to 17.67%, suggesting H2 improvement but a full-year deceleration in earnings quality. At a trailing P/E of 47×, the stock trades at the upper end of the Industrials sector benchmark range (28–45×), though the forward P/E of 33.8× — a meaningful 28% discount to trailing — signals the market is pricing in meaningful earnings recovery in FY27, supported by management's raised guidance of 12–13% volume growth and 15–17% piping volume growth; at P29 on forward P/E vs sector peers, it looks fairly valued to modestly cheap on a forward basis, but the EV/EBITDA of 29.5× still sits at P29 vs sector median of 34.4×, confirming no obvious mispricing. The single biggest risk is that PVC raw material price volatility persists into FY27, crimping the margin recovery management has guided for, especially given the still-integrating Wavin acquisition and a broader industry piping volume decline of 9% in FY26 that the company outgrew only through share gains.

fundamental.thesis: Astral Limited is a leading pipes, fittings, and adhesives manufacturer in India, with a strong brand in CPVC plumbing and a growing paints/adhesives segment that positions it in the residential and industrial construction value chain. The yfinance quarterly data shows Q4FY26 revenue of ₹1,248 cr (+22.5% YoY vs Q4FY25's ₹1,019 cr) and PAT of ₹213 cr (+18.8% YoY), which represents a sequential recovery from a weak Q3FY26 (₹925 cr revenue, ₹108 cr PAT); however, a contradictory filing extract reports consolidated revenue of ₹17,347 cr with a -45% YoY decline, which appears to reflect a different entity or reporting scope and cannot be reconciled without the full MD&A — this materially degrades data confidence. Trailing P/E of 76.5× sits at P88 among Industrials peers and is dramatically above the sector median benchmark of 28–45×, making the stock expensive on any conventional metric; forward P/E of 45.3× implies meaningful earnings growth is already priced in, and the 10.1× P/B with a modest 13.8% ROE does not justify a premium of this magnitude. The biggest risk is that elevated valuation leaves no margin of safety if volume growth in pipes decelerates — rising crude ($93/bbl) directly pressures PVC/CPVC raw material costs, squeezing margins at a time when the stock is priced for perfection.

fundamental.thesis: SRF is a diversified specialty chemicals and performance materials conglomerate with leading positions in fluorochemicals (refrigerants, HFCs), specialty chemicals (agrochemicals, pharma intermediates), performance films (BOPET/BOPP), and technical textiles — a unique multi-segment industrial platform in India. FY26 full-year results show revenue of ₹15,786 cr (+7.4% YoY) with PAT of ₹1,835 cr (+46.7% YoY), driven primarily by the Chemicals segment (+16% YoY revenue, +35.9% EBITDA growth to 29.1% margins) and a recovering Performance Films segment (+39.2% EBIT YoY); Q4FY26 showed continued momentum with quarterly revenue of ₹2,282 cr and PAT of ₹582 cr, both the strongest quarters of the year, while the PAT surge reflects operational leverage and a favourable ₹99 cr ITAT tax credit in Q3 that partially inflates the YoY PAT comparison. At a trailing P/E of 43.7× (P100 in the Materials sector peer set, +42% premium to sector median of 30.8×) and EV/EBITDA of 23.6× (+78% premium to sector median of 13.2×), SRF is unambiguously expensive relative to its Materials sector peers, though the premium partly reflects its specialty-chemicals quality versus commodity peers (Hindalco, Vedanta); the forward P/E of 30.1× suggests earnings growth is priced in but still carries a 30% premium to sector forward median. The single biggest risk is persistent Chinese competitive pricing pressure in both specialty chemicals and performance films, which — combined with U.S. tariff uncertainty and the indefinite deferral of the Indore BOPP facility — could stall the earnings recovery and leave the stock exposed at a peak-cycle valuation.

fundamental.thesis: Deepak Nitrite is a vertically integrated specialty chemicals manufacturer with leading positions in phenolics (acetone/phenol) and advanced intermediates (nitrites, nitration, amines), serving domestic and export markets. Q4FY26 showed a sharp sequential recovery — revenue of ₹2,127 cr (-3% YoY) with EBITDA surging 74% QoQ to ₹383 cr (18% margin) and PAT growing 9% YoY to ₹220 cr — but the full-year FY26 picture is materially weaker: revenue fell 5% YoY to ₹7,947 cr and PAT declined 21% YoY to ₹551 cr, reflecting sustained pricing pressure from Chinese dumping and global supply-chain disruptions throughout the year. Trailing P/E of 41.6× sits well above the Materials sector median of 12–22×, and EV/EBITDA of 24.6× is the most expensive in its peer group (P100), commanding a 33% premium to sector median EV/EBITDA of 18.5× — warranting an 'expensive' grade despite the forward P/E of 27.6× implying a recovery; ROE of only 9.8% with Debt/Equity of 27.9 does not justify this premium relative to materials peers. The biggest risk is that Chinese overcapacity and dumping persist beyond FY27, preventing the margin and earnings recovery that the current premium valuation demands, especially with the Nitric Acid plant still at ~45% utilisation and geopolitical freight disruptions ongoing.

fundamental.thesis: Tata Chemicals is a global soda ash and specialty chemicals producer (India, US, UK, Kenya) with a growing non-soda ash portfolio (bicarb, silica, salt, Rallis agri-inputs) that now contributes ~48% of FY26 consolidated revenue of ₹14,584 cr. Revenue declined 2% YoY in FY26 and EBITDA fell 8% to ₹1,805 cr (12.4% margin, down 60bps), while reported PAT collapsed 49.7% to ₹241 cr on a pre-exceptional basis — and turned deeply negative at the consolidated level due to a ₹1,837 cr US goodwill impairment and ₹182 cr deferred tax write-off; Q4FY26 standalone PAT was -₹279 cr before exceptionals versus -₹12 cr a year earlier, a sharp sequential and YoY deterioration driven by soda ash pricing at multi-year lows and elevated global inventories. EV/EBITDA of 14.5× sits modestly below the sector peer median of 16.5× (P43 rank) and well below the specialty/materials premium peers, while P/B of 0.82× is at P0 (cheapest in sector), but these apparently cheap multiples are mechanically suppressed by deeply negative trailing ROE (-7.7%) and a leveraged balance sheet (net debt ₹5,961 cr, D/E 36×), making them unreliable value signals rather than genuine cheapness. The single biggest risk is a prolonged soda ash down-cycle: Chinese domestic prices are down ~54% from Q3FY23 peaks, global inventories remain elevated at ~1.8 MT, and management has explicitly guided for flat near-term demand — any further price erosion or delay in the cycle recovery would extend losses in the US and UK segments, threaten interest coverage on the high debt load, and could trigger additional impairments.

fundamental.thesis: APL Apollo Tubes is India's largest structural steel tubes manufacturer, commanding dominant brand equity and a differentiated value-added product mix (57% VA sales in 9MFY26) that drives above-sector EBITDA/ton of ₹5,161–₹5,500. Trajectory is accelerating: Q4FY26 revenue grew to ₹5,268 cr vs ₹4,697 cr in Q4FY25 (+12% YoY), PAT grew strongly with FY26 full-year PAT up 59% YoY to ₹120 cr (as reported in the investor presentation), and management raised FY27 guidance to 15–20% volume growth, 20–25% EBITDA growth, and 25–30% PAT growth — a genuinely positive guidance arc from 'raised' in Q3FY26 to 'maintained' in Q4FY26. Valuation is expensive: trailing P/E of 42.2× sits at the P78 percentile vs Materials sector peers and is well above the 12–22× sector median for Materials, while EV/EBITDA of 27.7× is at P100 vs peers and 60% above the sector median of 17.3×, though the Forward P/E of 28.5× (a meaningful discount to trailing) and ROCE of 37% provide partial justification if FY27 guidance is delivered. Key risk is execution disruption: raw material (steel) shortages already constrained April 2026 production to 2.5 lakh tons vs a 7.92 lakh ton run-rate, Dubai operations are at only 40% utilisation due to the Middle East crisis, and the ₹1,400–1,500 cr pending capex for 8MT capacity expansion must be executed cleanly for the valuation premium to hold.

fundamental.thesis: Voltas is India's largest room air-conditioner brand (17.9% YTD market share, 5.1 percentage-point gap vs next competitor cluster) with a growing electro-mechanical projects (EMPS) order book of ₹6,200+ cr and an expanding home-appliances JV (Voltbek); however, FY26 has been a sharp deterioration year — full-year revenue declined 8.0% YoY to ₹14,483 cr and PAT collapsed 55.6% YoY to ₹370 cr, with Q4FY26 PAT down 52% YoY to ₹113 cr and UCP segment margins compressing 520bps from 8.4% to 3.2% due to commodity inflation (copper, aluminium) and INR depreciation. At a trailing P/E of 113.9× — a 33% premium to the sector median of 85.4× and at P100 (most expensive) among Consumer Discr. peers — and EV/EBITDA of 65×, the stock is unambiguously expensive relative to current earnings power; however, the forward P/E of 38.5× (at P0, cheapest in sector) prices in a substantial FY27 recovery anchored by management guidance of 15-20% RAC growth and GST rate cut tailwinds. The biggest risk is that the FY27 margin recovery narrative fails to materialise — if commodity costs remain elevated or INR depreciates further, the P/E re-rating story collapses and the stock, priced for perfection on forward earnings, has significant downside.

fundamental.thesis: Jubilant FoodWorks is India's dominant QSR operator running Domino's Pizza across 1,900+ stores, with a growing international presence via Turkey (DPEU) and emerging brands (Popeyes, Hong's); it holds clear market leadership in organised pizza delivery. Revenue trajectory is improving — consolidated top-line grew ~13% YoY in Q3FY26 and standalone FY26 revenue grew ~13% YoY to ₹686 cr, but the critical concern is decelerating LFL momentum: Q4FY26 LFL collapsed to just 0.2% with the 2-year CAGR falling from 9% to 6%, and PAT margin (standalone) remains thin at ~3.3%, further burdened by a one-off ₹337 cr Labour Code exceptional charge in Q3FY26 that distorts the reported numbers. At a trailing P/E of ~75× (well above the Consumer Discretionary/QSR benchmark of 40–60×) with a forward P/E of ~45× suggesting meaningful earnings recovery priced in, and EV/EBITDA at just ~14.9× (P0, cheapest in sector peer group) reflecting the Ind AS 116 lease distortion on PAT, the stock sits at a premium on earnings multiples but looks relatively cheap on cash-earnings basis — yet at only 5.6% of its 52-week range near a multi-year low, the market is clearly pricing in execution risk, not recovery. The single biggest risk is that LFL growth fails to recover toward the 5–7% management target while cost headwinds (energy +100–120 bps, labour +40–50 bps from minimum wages and Labour Code) compress already thin margins, making the forward P/E compression thesis dependent on a demand recovery that Q4 data does not yet support.

fundamental.thesis: United Breweries (UBL) is India's dominant beer company with the Kingfisher franchise, operating in a structurally attractive but highly regulated FMCG category with a premiumization tailwind — YTD premium volume growth of +23% is the standout positive from the Q3FY26 investor presentation. Revenue trajectory shows modest growth: Q3FY26 YTD revenue was ₹2,071 cr (+4.0% YoY), PAT surged +111% YoY to ₹81 cr largely on gross margin expansion of 222 bps to 45.3% (a 3-year high), though Q3 overall volumes declined 1.3% due to seasonal weather — the revenue growth has decelerated significantly from earlier quarters, and the North region (a key market) was down 8% YTD, signalling uneven recovery. Valuation is unambiguously expensive: trailing P/E of 84.3x is well above the FMCG sector median of 40–60x and UBL ranks P100 (most expensive) among NIFTY 100 FMCG peers on both trailing P/E (+51% premium to peer median) and EV/EBITDA at 45.3x (+31% to median), while ROE is a weak 9.3% against a P/B of 8.0x — a classic value-trap setup; the forward P/E of 45.2x implies substantial earnings growth is priced in, leaving little margin of safety. The biggest risk is that the valuation leaves zero room for error: any volume disappointment, regulatory price cap, or sustained cost inflation (crude at $93 is a direct input cost headwind via barley/packaging) could cause a sharp de-rating, and the 512% YTD increase in finance costs signals rising leverage pressure that could weigh on already thin PAT margins.

fundamental.thesis: P&G Hygiene & Health Care (PGHH) is a focused personal care and hygiene company (Whisper, Vicks, Oral-B) with a dominant market position in its niche categories, reporting FY26 full-year revenue of ₹4,290 cr (+27.2% YoY) and PAT of ₹856 cr (+34.8% YoY) at a 20% margin — though the YoY comparison is distorted by the prior period being only 9 months due to a financial year-end change, making the headline growth rates optically flattering and not directly comparable. On a more reliable 9M basis, revenue growth was a muted +1% YoY while PAT grew +25% YoY driven by operating leverage and cost discipline, suggesting the underlying top-line momentum is modest even as profitability improves — this divergence (volume/revenue stalling, margin expanding) aligns with the FMCG premiumisation risk where value growth masks weak real demand. Trailing P/E of 35.2× sits well below the FMCG sector median of ~40–60× and is the cheapest among peers at P0 percentile on both P/E and EV/EBITDA (24.8× vs sector median 33.9×), which is a genuine valuation signal, though the stock is near its 52-week low (8.3% of range) suggesting the market is pricing in earnings risk, not just apathy. The biggest risk is that the 27% revenue growth figure is a comparability illusion — if underlying organic volume growth remains near zero, the premium valuation re-rating thesis collapses and the stock stays a value trap despite cheap P/E multiples.

fundamental.thesis: Godrej Properties is India's largest listed residential developer by booking value, with a dominant pan-India presence across MMR, NCR, Bengaluru, Pune, and Hyderabad, achieving top-two rank in all five key markets. FY26 delivered record booking value of ₹34,171 cr (+16% YoY), PAT of ₹1,850 cr (+48% YoY), and Q4FY26 revenue of ₹3,895 cr (+47% YoY) with PAT of ₹650 cr (+70% YoY) — the trajectory is firmly accelerating across both booking volumes and recognised earnings, underpinned by a 41% CAGR in bookings over three years. At trailing P/E of 27.7×, GODREJPROP trades exactly at the sector median (20–35× benchmark range for Real Estate) and at a 15% discount to peers on forward P/E (16.1× vs median 19.0×), with P/B of 2.7× below the peer median of 3.2×, placing it at P0 (cheapest in sector) on both forward P/E and P/B — graded fair-to-cheap given FY27 guidance of ₹39,000 cr bookings (+14%) and collections of ₹24,000 cr (+20%), though the dividend yield figure of 59% in the snapshot appears anomalous and should not be relied upon. The biggest risk is the high debt/equity of 82x (absolute net debt ₹6,873 cr as of Q3FY26) combined with a negative EV/EBITDA in the snapshot suggesting balance-sheet complexity from the ₹6,000 cr QIP utilisation for land acquisition, while geopolitical disruptions (~₹1,000 cr Q4 sales impact), NCR project slippages, and real estate revenue recognition lumpiness (Q3 revenue -17% YoY despite strong bookings) create meaningful earnings-per-quarter volatility.

fundamental.thesis: Oberoi Realty is a premium Mumbai-focused residential and commercial real estate developer with a strong brand in the luxury segment, consistently commanding high realisation per square foot. All quarterly yfinance rows are marked DATA_SUSPECT (PAT > Revenue, likely standalone vs consolidated mismatch), and the FY26 annual report filing is unreadable due to encoding corruption while the investor presentation and earnings call transcripts contain no extractable financial data — so no reliable multi-quarter trajectory can be constructed from filings, preventing any revenue/PAT growth rate citation. On valuation, the trailing P/E of 23.7× sits comfortably below the sector benchmark range of 20–35× and is the cheapest among listed Real Estate peers at P0 percentile (12.7% discount to sector median), with a forward P/E of 15.4× representing a meaningful discount to trailing — implying the market expects earnings to grow; however, debt/equity of 15.77× is unusually high and demands close scrutiny of interest coverage, which cannot be assessed without clean financials. The primary risk to this thesis is the combination of high leverage in a rising crude/cost environment, the sector's underweight macro tilt, and the complete absence of verifiable filing data, which makes it impossible to confirm whether the optically attractive valuation reflects genuine earnings momentum or a deterioration in underlying business performance.

fundamental.thesis: Petronet LNG is India's largest LNG regasification company, operating the Dahej terminal (17.5 MMTPA now expanded to 22.5 MMTPA) and Kochi terminal, with a near-monopoly position in India's LNG import infrastructure. Q4FY26 delivered a PAT of ₹1,338 cr (+25.1% YoY, highest-ever quarterly PAT), but this was flattered by a one-off ₹550 cr UOP provision reversal and a ₹630 cr UOP cash receipt — the underlying picture is weaker, with 9M FY26 PAT of ₹2,505 cr down 12.3% YoY and Dahej utilisation collapsing to 53% in March 2026 due to Qatar supply disruption from the ongoing Middle East conflict. At a trailing P/E of 10.3× (vs sector median 12–14×) and EV/EBITDA of 6.1× (vs sector median ~11.6×, making it the cheapest in its peer group), the stock screens as cheap, but the discount is partly deserved given near-term earnings uncertainty from supply disruptions and a heavy ₹9,000 cr capex programme in FY27 dominated by the unproven petchem project. The single biggest risk is that Qatar LNG supplies do not resume on schedule, keeping Dahej utilisation suppressed into H1FY27 and materially denting FY27 earnings, while the high D/E of 10.5× limits balance sheet flexibility if the petchem capex cycle strains cash flows.

fundamental.thesis: ONGC is India's largest state-owned integrated oil and gas exploration and production company, commanding a dominant share of domestic crude and natural gas output. The 9MFY26 trend shows revenue declining -6.2% YoY and PAT declining -10.0% YoY to ₹26,244 cr with a PAT margin of 27.2%, reflecting pressure from softer realizations and elevated crude import costs feeding through the value chain — trajectory is weakening, not stabilising. Valuation is unambiguously cheap: trailing P/E of 8.1× sits well below the Energy sector median of 8–14× (at the low end), EV/EBITDA of 4.57× is deeply compressed for a capital-intensive E&P business, and P/B of 0.9× against ROE of 12.7% signals the market is pricing the book at a discount despite adequate returns — forward P/E of 6.13× implies the market expects earnings recovery, which is a positive asymmetry. The biggest risk is that crude oil at $94.89/bbl with INR at 95.71 creates a cost-squeeze on downstream subsidy-sharing obligations, and any government-directed under-recovery burden or production plateau from aging fields could keep earnings depressed despite the apparent valuation support.

fundamental.thesis: Bajaj Finserv is a diversified financial conglomerate holding stakes in Bajaj Finance (lending), Bajaj Allianz General Insurance, Bajaj Allianz Life Insurance, and an emerging mutual fund/AMC business, now 100% owned in insurance after buying out Allianz in Q4 FY26. Consolidated revenue grew +13.2% YoY to ₹1,50,530 cr in FY26 with PAT up +10.5% YoY to ₹9,801 cr at a 6.5% PAT margin — a single data point in the trend table limits trajectory confidence, but the 10-year CAGR of ~17% in both revenue and PAT, combined with a long-range FY30 aspiration of ~20% CAGR to $34–37bn total income, signals management's confidence in sustained compounding. Trailing P/E of 29× sits at P58 among 13 Financials peers and is ~46% above the sector median of 19.8×, placing BAJAJFINSV in 'fair-to-slightly-expensive' territory on earnings multiples, though EV/EBITDA of 12.1× is actually the cheapest in sector (P0), and forward P/E of 19.2× (well below trailing) implies meaningful earnings growth is expected, partially justifying the premium; P/B of 3.6× against ROE of 14.6% is elevated but not egregious for a high-quality franchise. The key risk is the very high debt/equity of 256×, primarily structural to the NBFC lending subsidiary (BFL), but in a rising crude/weak rupee/risk-off macro environment, any deterioration in BFL's credit quality or a sustained NPA uptick (currently Gross NPA 1.01%, Net NPA 0.41%) could compress valuations sharply.

fundamental.thesis: Coal India is India's dominant state-owned coal producer with a near-monopoly on domestic coal supply, serving power, steel, and cement sectors. FY26 annual results show a marginal revenue decline of -0.5% YoY (₹168,400 cr vs ₹169,177 cr) and a more concerning PAT decline of -12.3% YoY (₹31,071 cr vs ₹35,450 cr), with PAT margin at 18.4% — a one-year snapshot but directionally negative, complicated by a retrospective accounting restatement and a ₹1,458 cr executive pay provision that partly explains the PAT compression. At a trailing P/E of 9.31× and EV/EBITDA of 6.07×, the stock sits firmly below the Energy sector median of 8–14× on P/E and well below on EV/EBITDA, marking it cheap on absolute valuation — however, the stock is at 82.6% of its 52-week range, limiting near-term risk/reward, and forward P/E of 8.22× implies modest earnings recovery is already priced in. The biggest risk is structural: accelerating India energy transition and renewable capacity additions could suppress long-run thermal coal demand, while near-term governance concerns (non-compliance with independent director requirements incurring exchange penalties) add regulatory overhang.

fundamental.thesis: Asian Paints is India's dominant decorative paints company with ~50% market share, operating in a structurally growing but increasingly competitive market; the business has diversified into industrial coatings, home décor, and construction chemicals. Revenue growth has been modest at +3.9% YoY in Q3FY26 (9M basis), with decorative volume growth of 10.9% partially offset by ~1% price deflation, while PAT on a standalone Q3FY26 basis actually declined -4.5% YoY to ₹1,060 cr — a concerning divergence between volume and earnings momentum. At a trailing P/E of 58.95×, Asian Paints trades far above the Materials sector median of 12–22×; even accounting for its FMCG-like brand positioning, this is expensive relative to the sector and peers, with EV/EBITDA at 37.67× confirming elevated pricing for a business growing revenues at under 4%. The biggest risk is sustained margin and earnings pressure from competitive intensity — new entrants (Birla Opus) are driving pricing discipline and discounting, while the home décor segment remains a drag, and the debt/equity of 17.85× warrants close monitoring of interest coverage.

fundamental.thesis: Bajaj Auto is India's leading two- and three-wheeler manufacturer with a dominant position in the 125cc+ motorcycle segment, a rapidly scaling EV business (Chetak 2W and E-3W), and a now-consolidated controlling stake in KTM/Bajaj Mobility AG giving it a premium global motorcycle platform. Revenue trajectory shows genuine acceleration — Q4FY26 delivered +32% YoY revenue growth (₹16,006 cr) and +34% YoY PAT growth (₹2,746 cr), with FY26 full-year revenue of ₹62,905 cr (+23.4% YoY) and PAT of ₹10,744 cr (+46.6% YoY), PAT margins expanding from 16.4% in Q3FY26 to 17.1% in Q4FY26 and maintained at 17.1% for full-year FY26 — the EV segment reaching double-digit EBITDA margins for the first time is a structural inflection worth noting. At a trailing P/E of 26.79× against the Auto sector benchmark of 18–28×, Bajaj Auto sits in the upper-fair zone — not expensive in isolation, but the stock is at 82.1% of its 52-week range (near ₹10,834 high), which prices in much of the near-term execution and leaves limited margin of safety; forward P/E of 22.14× (a meaningful discount to trailing) signals the market expects continued earnings growth, but the EV/EBITDA of 19.7× is comfortably within sector norms for a capital-light auto franchise. The biggest near-term risk is the Q1FY27 cost inflation squeeze — management flagged 3.5–4% of revenue in commodity cost headwinds (some inputs up 15–45%) with pricing recovering only ~40% of that impact, alongside motorcycle category growth decelerating sharply from 20% to 7–9%, meaning PAT margins could compress meaningfully in the next one or two quarters.

fundamental.thesis: Adani Green Energy is India's largest renewable energy developer with 19.3 GW of operational capacity and a globally significant project at Khavda, commanding a near-monopoly scale in utility-scale solar/wind under long-term PPAs (~90%+ coverage). Valuation is unambiguously expensive: trailing P/E of 145.5× is roughly 8–10× the Utilities sector median of 10–18×, and even EV/EBITDA of 32.24× sits above the upper end of that range; the stock at 84.4% of its 52-week range leaves little margin of safety, and P/B of 12.06× against a weak ROE of 7.6% signals that the market is paying a large speculative premium for future growth rather than current returns. fundamental.thesis: Canara Bank is India's third-largest public sector bank by advances (₹12.38L cr), with a growing retail lending franchise that surged 32.93% YoY to ₹2.97L cr in FY26. FY26 full-year PAT grew 12.7% YoY to ₹19,187 cr on revenue growth of 7.7% YoY (₹1.53L cr), with credit quality markedly improved — Gross NPA fell 110 bps to 1.84%, Net NPA at 0.43%, PCR at 94.21%, and credit cost compressed 33 bps to 0.59%; loan growth of 15.3% YoY beat management's own 10–11% guidance, and ROA reached 1.1% against a >1% target. At a trailing P/E of 6.07× and P/B of 1.02× against a sector median P/E range of 12–18× for banks, with ROE of 15.9%, the stock screens as deeply cheap on both P/E and P/B frameworks — a P/B below 1.5 with ROE well above 12% is a textbook undervaluation signal for a PSU bank. The primary risk is NIM compression: management has guided NIM at 2.5–2.6% (FY26 NIM was 2.51%), with a low CASA ratio of 29.86% leaving the bank more vulnerable to deposit repricing as the RBI rate-cut cycle progresses.

fundamental.thesis: Jindal Steel & Power (JINDALSTEL) is a large integrated steel producer with 15.6 MTPA steelmaking capacity, currently ramping up its Angul greenfield expansion and among the largest private-sector steel players in India. FY26 annual revenue grew +8.0% YoY to ₹62,412 cr with PAT up +18.1% to ₹3,361 cr (PAT margin 5.4%), both metrics stable rather than accelerating — however, the key sector KPI of EBITDA per tonne deteriorated from ₹11,712 in FY25 to ₹10,482 in FY26, signalling cost-side pressure from coking coal that offset volume gains from capacity ramp-up. On valuation, trailing P/E of 36.35× looks expensive versus the Materials sector median of 12–22×, but forward P/E of 12.55× implies the market is pricing in a sharp earnings recovery — EV/EBITDA of 14.46× is more relevant for this capital-intensive business and sits at the upper end of the sector range, suggesting fair-to-slightly-elevated pricing with a meaningful ROE of only 6.8% on high D/E of 43.61×. The biggest risk is that coking coal costs rise a further $20–25/tonne in Q1FY27 as guided by management, while the slurry pipeline savings and DRI2/PP2 commissioning benefits are back-half weighted, creating a near-term earnings squeeze that could disappoint the optimistic forward P/E implied by the market.

fundamental.thesis: Berger Paints is India's second-largest decorative paints company with a strong retail distribution network, competing directly with Asian Paints and facing increasing competition from Grasim/Birla Opus. The latest available quarterly data (Q3FY26) shows revenue of ₹1,699 cr, a YoY decline of -2.2% vs Q3FY25's ₹1,737 cr, while the full-year FY26 revenue grew a modest +2.5% to ₹10,420 cr — a significant deceleration from the mid-to-high single digit growth rates of prior years; critically, PAT margin at the full-year level is 10.5% but Q4FY26 standalone PAT margin collapsed to just 4.3% (₹107 cr on ₹2,504 cr revenue), suggesting margin pressure is intensifying rather than abating. Trailing P/E of 51.8× sits far above the Materials sector median of 39.1× (P82 in sector) and well above the sector benchmark range of 12–22×, with EV/EBITDA of 29.8× representing a 105% premium to the sector median of 14.5× — making BERGEPAINT expensive on every metric, second only to Asian Paints and Pidilite in its peer group. The single biggest risk is that the entry of Grasim/Birla Opus with significant capex-backed capacity expansion accelerates market share erosion precisely when crude-oil-derived raw material costs are rising (WTI at $105) and volume growth is already soft, threatening both the top line and the premium valuation multiple.

fundamental.thesis: Indian Bank is a mid-sized public sector bank (73.84% GoI-owned) with a diversified advances book — RAM (Retail, Agriculture, MSME) growing at ~15% YoY and Corporate at ~9% YoY — delivering FY26 PAT of ₹12,156 cr (+11.3% YoY) on NII of ₹26,915 cr (+6.9% YoY), though Q4FY26 quarterly PAT of ₹3,103 cr was only +1.3% QoQ, suggesting a plateauing quarterly run-rate. Asset quality has improved sharply — GNPA at 1.98% vs 3.09% a year ago and NNPA at just 0.15% — but NIM has compressed 17 bps YoY (3.41% to 3.24%), RoE has declined 203 bps YoY to 18.98%, and cost-to-income has risen to 46.03%, indicating margin and efficiency headwinds that offset credit-quality gains. At trailing P/E of 9.87× and P/B of 1.44× with ROE of 15.4%, the stock screens as cheap vs both the sector benchmark (Financials 12–18×) and peers (P21 percentile, 42% discount to sector median P/E), consistent with a PSU bank discount, though the forward P/E of 7.99× confirms the market prices in continued earnings growth. The principal risk is further NIM compression — management's own FY27 NIM guidance of 3.10–3.25% signals a step-down from current levels — which, combined with cost-of-deposit stickiness and a moderating RoE trajectory, could cap re-rating despite the cheap absolute valuation.

fundamental.thesis: Coforge is a mid-tier Indian IT services company focused on Banking & Financial Services, Insurance, Travel, and Healthcare verticals, with a strengthened AI and digital engineering proposition following the Encora acquisition and Cigniti merger. FY26 full-year revenue grew a strong 35.9% YoY to ₹16,421 cr (₹164,207 mn), with EBITDA margin expanding 431 bps to 18.6% and PAT of ₹1,556 cr (PAT margin ~9.5%) — however, the PAT figure includes an estimated ~₹166 cr one-off benefit from a Cigniti deferred tax liability reversal, and Q3FY26 PAT of ₹250 cr (per yfinance) vs Q4FY26 ₹612 cr suggests significant quarterly lumpiness, warranting caution on normalised earnings power; revenue growth trajectory is clearly accelerating (Q3FY25 ~₹1,959 cr to Q4FY26 ~₹2,755 cr sequentially), driven partly by inorganic consolidation. Trailing P/E of 33× sits at the upper end of the IT sector median range of 22–30× and is 87% above the sector peer median of 17.7×, placing it at P80 in the peer set — expensive on earnings multiples — though the forward P/E of 21× (a meaningful discount to trailing) suggests the market is pricing in continued growth, and management has raised FY27 guidance for EBITDA margins above 20.5%; the P/B of 5.1× is broadly in line with peers at P40, and ROE of 18.6% is acceptable but not exceptional for this valuation. The single biggest risk is that the reported PAT and EPS are materially distorted by one-off merger accounting items (Cigniti deferred tax reversal), making normalised earnings difficult to assess, while a high debt/equity of 7.52 and rising crude/INR headwinds on discretionary tech spend compound uncertainty for a midcap name with thinner liquidity.

fundamental.thesis: IPCA Laboratories is a vertically integrated Indian pharma company with a strong domestic formulations franchise (ranked #16 in IPM) and growing export presence across generics (US/Europe), APIs, and branded ROW markets, benefiting from the China+1 tailwind in API supply chains. FY26 consolidated revenue grew 7.9% YoY to ₹9,646 cr, with PAT surging 54.8% YoY to ₹1,141 cr (partly aided by prior-year exceptional charges skewing the base); EBITDA margin improved 78 bps to 20.7%, and management has raised FY27 guidance to 12-13% revenue growth with EBITDA margins of ~22-22.3%, a meaningful step-up that signals genuine operating leverage ahead. Trailing P/E of 36.2× sits at P33 in the sector — a slight discount to the peer median of 41.3× and inside the 28-40× pharma benchmark range, with forward P/E of 24.9× materially below trailing, suggesting the market is already pricing in the FY27 earnings acceleration; on balance this is fair with a slight cheap tilt, but the stock is near its 52-week high (88.9% of range), leaving limited margin of safety at current prices. The biggest risk is that PAT growth in FY26 was heavily flattered by a low prior-year base (FY25 had ₹205 cr of exceptional charges) and that rising material costs (solvents up 40-50%, packaging materials up sharply) combined with Unichem's weak margins (8% EBITDA, down from 12%) could compress the anticipated FY27 margin expansion if management's offset assumptions on product mix do not materialise.

fundamental.thesis: Dixon Technologies is India's largest Electronics Manufacturing Services (EMS) player, a direct beneficiary of the China+1 supply-chain shift and PLI incentives, with dominant positions in mobile/smartphone assembly, consumer electronics, and a rapidly scaling IT hardware and camera module business. FY26 consolidated revenue grew 26% YoY to ₹48,893 cr and PAT grew 20% YoY to ₹845 cr, but the trajectory has softened sequentially — Q4FY26 EBITDA margin compressed to 4.0% vs 5.1% in 9M FY26, driven by memory chip inflation, PLI phase-out headwinds (0.5–0.6% margin drag), inventory rationalisation by brands, and geopolitical supply disruptions; adjusted (ex-exceptional) PAT growth is lower than reported, and Q3FY26 PAT was actually down 1.4% YoY. At a trailing P/E of 42.9× (at sector median for Industrials peers) and EV/EBITDA of 32.3× (below sector median of 36.5×, P33), valuation is broadly fair — the 12.9× P/B is elevated but defensible given ROE of 37.1% and ROCE of 44.8%, though Forward P/E of 45.1× slightly above trailing signals near-term earnings pressure. The single biggest risk is sustained margin compression from memory price inflation and PLI uncertainty coinciding with the ramp-up capex cycle for HKC display JV and Q Tech camera modules, where execution delays or cost overruns could impair FCF (FY26 CFO was negative) and stress the high D/E of 18.46×.

fundamental.thesis: BHEL is India's dominant state-owned heavy electrical equipment manufacturer, commanding the power-sector capital goods space with a near-monopoly in thermal and hydro power plant equipment and a growing presence in defence, railways, and industrial segments. FY26 full-year revenue grew +19.2% YoY to ₹33,782 cr with PAT surging +207.7% YoY to ₹1,578 cr (PAT margin expanding sharply from 1.81% to 4.67%), and Q3FY26 showed consistent momentum at +16.4% revenue growth and +206.6% PAT growth YoY — a clear multi-quarter recovery trajectory driven by the government capex/power cycle tailwind. However, at a trailing P/E of 84.3× versus the Industrials sector median of 28–45×, BHEL is deeply expensive in absolute terms and sits at P89 in its peer group on trailing P/E and EV/EBITDA of 56.2× (vs peer median 37.5×); forward P/E of 29.7× implies the market is already pricing in very aggressive earnings normalisation, leaving little margin of safety at 82.6% of its 52-week range. The single biggest risk is that BHEL's structural operating leverage is real but fragile — ROE remains a weak 6.3% with high debt/equity of 31.3×, EBITDA margins are still thin at ~7%, and any slowdown in government power-sector order placement or execution delays would rapidly erode a valuation that is priced for near-perfection.

fundamental.thesis: Coromandel International is India's second-largest phosphatic fertilizer producer with a growing crop protection (agrochemicals) business, benefiting from backward integration into phosphoric and sulphuric acid at Kakinada. Q4FY26 consolidated revenue grew +19% YoY to ₹6,068 cr, a healthy top-line; however, PAT collapsed 80% YoY to ₹115 cr (1.9% margin) due to a one-off ₹71 cr impairment on Dhaksha investments versus a ₹347 cr land-sale gain in Q4FY25 — stripping these, underlying earnings were still compressed by ammonia ($840-850/ton) and sulphur ($800/ton) cost spikes squeezing fertilizer EBITDA/ton from ₹5,000+ to ~₹3,500, while full-year FY26 PAT of ₹1,898 cr on revenue of ₹31,827 cr (+30% YoY) shows the franchise remains structurally intact and growing. On valuation, trailing P/E of 27.2× sits in the lower-mid range of the 12–22× Materials sector benchmark (slightly above it), but forward P/E of 18.0× — a 34% discount to trailing — signals the market is pricing in meaningful earnings recovery; at P22 in the peer group on forward P/E, Coromandel is among the cheaper names in Materials, though its P/B of 4.2× (P78 in sector) is elevated relative to peers, making it fair-to-slightly-expensive overall. The single biggest risk is sustained raw material inflation (ammonia, sulphur, rock phosphate) combined with inadequate or delayed government subsidy revision, which directly compresses fertilizer EBITDA/ton and can overwhelm the crop protection growth story.

FY26 full-year PAT grew 14% YoY to ₹10,527 cr on 6% revenue growth, though Q4FY26 standalone PAT of ~₹3,088 cr marks a sequential recovery; critically, NIM compressed 30 bps YoY to 2.52% (from 2.82%) reflecting repo rate transmission pressure on the 64% EBLR-linked book, while asset quality improved meaningfully — Gross NPA fell 129 bps to 1.98% and slippage ratio halved to 0.83% from 1.36%. At a trailing P/E of 6.0×, the stock trades at a 65% discount to the Financials sector median of 17× and sits at the P0 percentile on P/B (0.72× vs sector median 1.9×) with ROE of 12.1%, firmly in 'cheap' territory by any peer-relative or absolute measure. The single biggest risk is sustained NIM compression — management's own FY27 NIM guidance of 2.70–2.75% (domestic exit 2.80%) implies further near-term pressure before recovery, and any incremental rate cuts by RBI would widen the gap between stated targets and realised margins.

fundamental.thesis: L&T Technology Services (LTTS) is a pure-play engineering R&D services company — one of India's largest ER&D players — operating across Mobility, Sustainability, and Tech segments after divesting the SWC business in FY26. FY26 full-year revenue grew 14% YoY in INR terms (₹109,959 Mn) on a continuing-operations basis, but USD revenue growth was only 8.3% and Q4 FY26 USD revenue growth decelerated sharply to just 0.3% YoY ($305.9 Mn), while PAT grew a modest 7.4% YoY to ₹12,818 Mn at an 11.7% margin — the gap between INR and USD growth is largely an INR depreciation tailwind that is now reversing given INR strengthening to 95.35. Trailing P/E of 28.2× sits at P71 vs IT sector peers and is +29% above the sector median of 21.8×; the forward P/E of 20.0× implies earnings growth is expected, but the EV/EBITDA of 18.3× is at P80 vs peers, making the stock expensive-to-fair relative to the peer set despite the 52-week low proximity (stock at only 15.1% of its 52-week range at ₹3,010–₹4,726). The biggest risk is the Mobility segment drag (−5% YoY) compounded by GenAI disruption to the ER&D services model, while the Q4 USD revenue near-stagnation signals that the FY26 large-deal TCV momentum ($855 Mn, +40% YoY) has not yet converted to sustained revenue acceleration — if macro headwinds persist into H1 FY27, consensus earnings estimates (reflected in the forward P/E) could prove too optimistic.
